title: Herpes Simplex (Oral & Genital)
oneLiner: A lifelong viral infection causing recurrent painful vesicles/ulcers on oral or genital mucosa, with neonatal risk and rare CNS complications.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 22-year-old university student presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of grouped, painful blisters on her labia. She reports tingling and burning in the area 12 hours before the blisters appeared. She is otherwise well, no fever, and has had similar episodes twice in the past year. She is sexually active with a new partner. On examination, multiple 1–2 mm vesicles on an erythematous base are seen on the labia minora, with some rupturing to form shallow, tender ulcers. Inguinal lymph nodes are mildly enlarged and tender.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Grouped vesicles on an erythematous base** → pathognomonic for herpes simplex (or zoster); vesicles are intraepidermal.
- **Preceding tingling/burning** → prodromal symptom due to viral reactivation and nerve irritation (ganglion involvement).
- **Recurrent episodes** → latent virus in sacral ganglia reactivates periodically.
- **Sexually active + new partner** → typical epidemiology for genital HSV-2 (though HSV-1 increasingly causes genital herpes).
- **Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy** → local lymphatic spread; supports diagnosis and helps differentiate from painless chancres.
- **Shallow ulcers after vesicle rupture** → typical natural history; ulcers heal without scarring in immunocompetent patients.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Neonatal herpes** – vesicular rash in a newborn, but may present with fever, irritability, or sepsis-like picture without skin lesions in visceral/CNS forms. Maternal history often absent.
- **Eczema herpeticum** – widespread vesicles on pre-existing eczematous skin in patients with atopic dermatitis. Can be mistaken for worsening eczema.
- **Herpetic whitlow** – isolated painful vesiculopustular lesion on a finger (often in healthcare workers). Misdiagnosed as paronychia or bacterial felon.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Herpes simplex is a double-stranded DNA virus (HSV-1 and HSV-2) that causes primary infection of mucosal or abraded skin, then establishes lifelong latency in sensory ganglia. Reactivation leads to recurrent lesions at or near the original site.

Why it matters:

- **Extremely common** – Estimated 67% of people under 50 have HSV-1 (mostly oral), 13% have HSV-2 (mostly genital). Many are asymptomatic but shed virus.
- **Neonatal herpes** – rare but devastating (mortality up to 50% without treatment); requires immediate IV acyclovir.
- **Immunocompromised patients** – extensive, atypical, or chronic ulcerations; risk of dissemination.
- **Misdiagnosis** – oral herpes can be mistaken for aphthous ulcers (no vesicles), genital herpes for syphilis or chancroid; incorrect treatment can delay healing and increase transmission.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Primary infection:** The virus enters through breaks in mucosa or skin. It replicates in epithelial cells, causing ballooning degeneration and lysis – this produces the characteristic **vesicle** (intraepidermal blister). The vesicle roof is thin and quickly breaks, leaving a **shallow ulcer**.

**Spread to nervous system:** From the epithelium, virus enters sensory nerve endings and travels retrograde to the dorsal root ganglion (trigeminal for oral, sacral for genital). Here it becomes **latent** – no virus replication, but viral DNA persists indefinitely.

**Reactivation triggers:** Fever, UV light, menstruation, stress, trauma, or immunosuppression reactivate the virus. It travels anterograde back to the skin, causing identical lesions at the same site.

**Why the prodrome?** Before lesions appear, viral reactivation in the ganglion triggers neuropathic pain/tingling. This is a key early clue.

**Why recurrence?** Latency means the virus cannot be eliminated. Immune control (especially T-cell responses) keeps it suppressed, but periodic reactivation occurs even in healthy hosts.

**Why lymphadenopathy?** Viral replication in skin releases antigens that drain to regional lymph nodes, causing inflammation and enlargement.

**Neonatal transmission:** Typically during vaginal delivery through contact with infected genital secretions. Can lead to disseminated disease (liver, lung, brain) or isolated CNS disease.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Direct infection** | HSV-1 (typically oral), HSV-2 (typically genital) | Lesion site matches serotype pattern (though overlap increasing) |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Fever, sunlight, stress, menstruation, trauma | Patient reports “cold sore” after sunburn or exam stress |
| **Immunocompromised state** | HIV, chemotherapy, transplant, steroids | Chronic, extensive, or atypical ulcers; also high risk for acyclovir resistance |
| **Neonatal acquisition** | Maternal genital herpes at time of delivery | Newborn with vesicles, fever, or encephalitis within 2 weeks of life (type may be HSV-2) |
| **Iatrogenic / occupational** | Needlestick injury, surgery (e.g., trigeminal nerve procedures) | Healthcare worker with finger lesion – herpetic whitlow |

**Most common cause:** HSV-1 for oral herpes (90%+); HSV-2 for genital herpes (60–70% in developed countries, but HSV-1 genital rising to 40% in some populations due to oral-genital contact).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient – grouped vesicles on erythematous base + recurrence. Confirmation is recommended in: first episode (to type), severe/atypical cases, pregnancy, immunocompromised, neonates.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Viral culture** (swab from vesicle base) | Live virus in cell culture | Gold standard for typing; high specificity | Positive = active infection. Negative may be false if lesion is drying. |
| **PCR** (swab or CSF) | Viral DNA | Most sensitive; rapid; can type (HSV-1 vs 2) | Positive confirms. Useful for CSF in suspected encephalitis or neonatal disease. |
| **Tzanck smear** (scraping from vesicle base) | Multinucleated giant cells (syncytia) | Quick bedside test; cheap | Positive suggests herpes (any type) – cannot differentiate HSV from VZV. Low sensitivity. |
| **Serology** (IgG / IgM) | Antibodies to HSV | History of recurrent infection? Determine past exposure (type-specific) | IgM not useful for acute diagnosis. IgG positive indicates past infection. Rising IgG in paired sera can confirm primary. Useful in pregnancy if partner has herpes. |
| **CSF analysis** (if encephalitis suspected) | Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose; HSV PCR positive | Confirm HSV encephalitis | High viral load in CSF correlates with worse outcome. |

**Single finding that confirms:** Positive HSV PCR from a lesion or CSF. Clinical appearance (grouped vesicles) combined with positive PCR is essentially 100% specific.

**Single finding that rules out:** Negative HSV PCR from a well-sampled vesicle (especially if fresh) in an immunocompetent patient with typical lesions – consider alternative diagnoses (aphthous ulcer, zoster, etc.).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Assess severity:** Is patient immunocompromised? Pregnant? Neonate? Disseminated? Encephalitis?
- **If severe or complicated** – start intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours (neonates: 20 mg/kg every 8 hours) – covers HSV, reduces viral load, prevents progression.
- **If mild-moderate first episode** – oral antiviral (see below) reduces duration and severity.
- **Pain control:** simple analgesia (paracetamol, ibuprofen). Topical lidocaine 2% gel can be used for mucosal lesions.
- **Wound care:** Keep lesions clean and dry. Avoid tight clothing for genital lesions.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Antiviral therapy (oral) for first episode:**
  - Acyclovir 400 mg orally three times daily for 5–10 days OR
  - Valacyclovir 1 g orally twice daily for 5–10 days OR
  - Famciclovir 250 mg orally three times daily for 5–10 days.
- **Recurrent episodes (mild):** Can be treated with episodic therapy (short course) or suppressive therapy (if frequent).
  - Episodic: Valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily for 3 days; start at prodrome.
  - Suppressive (≥6 episodes/year): Valacyclovir 500 mg once daily (or 1 g if frequent genital HSV-2). Reduces recurrences by 70–80%.
- **Neonatal herpes:** IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days (depending on disease extent).
- **HSV encephalitis:** IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days; monitor renal function.
- **Pregnancy:** At term, consider prophylaxis (acyclovir 400 mg three times daily from 36 weeks) to reduce recurrence. Cesarean section recommended if active lesions at delivery.
- **HIV co-infection:** May need higher doses or longer courses; consider resistance testing if not improving.

**Long-term:**

- **Lifelong latency** – no cure. Focus on education: recognize prodrome, use condoms (reduces but does not eliminate transmission), avoid sexual contact during lesions.
- **Suppressive therapy** for frequent or severe recurrences (e.g., valacyclovir 500–1000 mg daily) – safe for long-term use.
- **Psychological support** – stigma is common; reassure that most people have HSV.

**What NOT to do:**

- **Do NOT use topical acyclovir for genital herpes** – oral therapy is far more effective. Topical is only an adjunct for oral herpes (and even then, oral is better).
- **Do NOT use steroids topically on herpes lesions** – can worsen and spread infection.
- **Do NOT assume all recurrent mouth ulcers are herpes** – aphthous ulcers are not vesicular and usually not painful at rest.
- **Do NOT delay acyclovir in suspected neonatal herpes** while waiting for culture – start IV acyclovir immediately.
- **Do NOT prescribe acyclovir for asymptomatic viral shedding without lesions** – no evidence of benefit for transmission reduction (unless partner is pregnant or immunocompromised – then discuss counseling).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Herpes Simplex (Oral/Genital) | Aphthous Ulcer (Oral) | Herpes Zoster (Shingles) | Primary Syphilis (Chancre) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp, burning, often preceded by tingling | Sharp, stinging; no prodrome | Burning, shooting, dermatomal pain before rash | Painless (may be mildly tender) |
| **Exam** | Grouped vesicles on erythematous base → shallow ulcers | Solitary or multiple round/oval ulcers with white/yellow base, red halo, no vesicles | Vesicles in dermatomal distribution (usually unilateral); crusts later | Single, painless, indurated ulcer with clean base; unilateral lymphadenopathy (rubbery) |
| **Labs** | HSV PCR positive; multinucleated giant cells on Tzanck | No specific test; biopsy if suspicious of Behçet’s | VZV PCR positive; Tzanck also shows multinucleated cells | Dark-field microscopy shows spirochetes; serology positive (VDRL/RPR/TPPA) |
| **Key differentiator** | Recurrence at same site; grouped vesicles | Recurrent but never vesicular; no systemic symptoms | Dermatomal distribution, unilateral, severe pain, often in older/immunocompromised | Painless ulcer + history of exposure; test for syphilis |
| **Immunocompromised** | Extensive, chronic ulcerations; acyclovir resistance possible | May be larger and more painful; no vesicles | Disseminated zoster; postherpetic neuralgia common | Atypical rashes, multiple chancres |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Neonatal herpes** (missed maternal lesions) | Newborn (day 3–14) with vesicles, lethargy, fever, or seizures | Vesicular rash; may have no rash (CNS) | Start IV acyclovir immediately; perform CSF PCR; consult pediatric ID |
| **Herpes encephalitis** | Fever, headache, confusion, focal neurological deficits (temporal lobe) | CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, RBCs may be present. MRI: temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity | IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h; EEG shows periodic lateralizing epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) |
| **Disseminated HSV** | Multi-organ failure, hepatitis, pneumonitis | Elevated transaminases, coagulopathy, ARDS; skin lesions may be absent | IV acyclovir; requires ICU; consider resistance testing |
| **Eczema herpeticum** | Rapid onset of painful vesicles over pre-existing eczema; fever, malaise | Widespread vesicles, may become pustular; risk of secondary bacterial infection | IV acyclovir; stop topical steroids; consider antibiotics if superinfected |
| **Herpetic whitlow** | Painful vesiculopustular lesion on finger, often in healthcare workers | Misdiagnosed as bacterial felon | Oral valacyclovir 1 g twice daily for 7–10 days; avoid incision and drainage (spreads virus) |
| **HSV keratitis** (ocular herpes) | Pain, photophobia, blurred vision; dendritic ulcer on slit lamp | Dendritic ulcer on cornea (pathognomonic) | Urgent ophthalmology referral; topical antivirals (e.g., acyclovir ointment); steroids contraindicated in epithelial disease |
| **Immunocompromised chronic ulcers** | Non-healing ulcers >1 month | Large, painful, raised borders; may be acyclovir-resistant | Consider IV foscarnet or cidofovir; ID consultation |
| **Transmission to partner** | Asymptomatic shedding accounts for most transmission | No lesions, but virus detectable by PCR | Suppressive therapy reduces shedding; counseling on condoms and disclosure |

**Late-presentation patterns (what happens if untreated):**  
- Primary genital herpes can last 2–4 weeks with viral shedding and severe pain → risk of secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis).  
- Herpes encephalitis untreated → rapid progression to coma, brain herniation, death within days.  
- Neonatal herpes untreated → mortality >80%; survivors often have severe neurological deficits.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **"SORES" for features of herpetic ulcer:**  
  S – Shallow ulcer  
  O – On red base  
  R – Recurrent  
  E – Epithelial (vesicle then ulcer)  
  S – Sheds virus (contagious)

- **"ACID" for antiviral choice in genital herpes (first episode, mild):**  
  A – Acyclovir 400 mg TDS  
  C – Cycles 5–10 days  
  I – Interferon (not used)  
  D – Don't forget valacyclovir/famciclovir as easier alternatives

- **"NEONATE" risk factors for neonatal herpes:**  
  N – No maternal history (often first episode)  
  E – Early delivery (before 38 weeks)  
  O – Obvious lesions at delivery  
  N – Not using acyclovir prophylaxis  
  A – Asymptomatic shedding  
  T – Type (HSV-2 more neurovirulent)  
  E – Emergency C-section

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Ulcers in the mouth that start as blisters? Think herpes – aphthous ulcers never have a vesicular stage.**  
2. **Genital ulcer that is painful = likely herpes or chancroid; painless = syphilis until proven otherwise.**  
3. **Recurrent rash on same finger in a nurse/dentist = herpetic whitlow until proven otherwise.**  
4. **Encephalitis with temporal lobe involvement + CSF RBCs = HSV until proven otherwise.**  
5. **Immunocompromised patient with chronic genital ulcers not healing with acyclovir → check for acyclovir resistance (usually in HIV with low CD4).**  
6. **First episode genital herpes can present with urinary retention (sacral radiculopathy) – catheterize and start antivirals.**  
7. **Neonatal rash plus lethargy: consider HSV even if no maternal history; start acyclovir immediately – do not wait for cultures.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking herpetic gingivostomatitis (primary oral HSV in children) for hand-foot-mouth disease (coxsackievirus) – HFMD has vesicles on palms/soles and no fever in prodrome.
- Prescribing topical steroids for presumed eczema when it's eczema herpeticum (Kaposi's varicelliform eruption).
- Ordering HSV serology for acute lesions – PCR is faster and more sensitive.
- Starting antibiotics for a herpes lesion – they do not treat viral infection and may cause superinfection.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior or involve other specialties:**

- **Suspected neonatal herpes** – immediate consult to pediatrician, pediatric ID, start IV acyclovir.
- **HSV encephalitis** – call neurology/ID; MRI brain, EEG, CSF PCR; ICU if GCS < 8 or airway compromise.
- **Disseminated HSV (hepatitis, pneumonitis, DIC)** – discuss with ICU and ID; monitor transaminases, INR, oxygen saturations.
- **Ocular herpes (dendritic ulcer)** – urgent ophthalmology review within hours.
- **Immunocompromised patient not responding to empiric acyclovir** – discuss ID for resistance testing (foscarnet therapy).
- **Pregnant woman at term with active genital lesions** – call obstetrics: plan C-section, start IV acyclovir, neonatology consult.

**Specific thresholds to trigger escalation:**

- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially if encephalopathic) – generate sepsis/neurological evaluation.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (can occur in disseminated HSV hepatitis).
- **GCS < 15 with fever and headache** – suspect encephalitis, call neurologist.
- **Altered mental status + seizures** – immediate neurology/ICU involvement.
- **Rising transaminases (ALT > 500) without clear cause** – consider HSV hepatitis in at-risk patients (immunocompromised, pregnant).

## Herpes Simplex (Oral & Genital)
**One-liner:** A lifelong viral infection causing recurrent painful vesicles/ulcers on oral or genital mucosa, with neonatal risk and rare CNS complications.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 22-year-old university student presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of grouped, painful blisters on her labia. She reports tingling and burning in the area 12 hours before the blisters appeared. She is otherwise well, no fever, and has had similar episodes twice in the past year. She is sexually active with a new partner. On examination, multiple 1–2 mm vesicles on an erythematous base are seen on the labia minora, with some rupturing to form shallow, tender ulcers. Inguinal lymph nodes are mildly enlarged and tender.

**Dissecting the vignette – pattern clues:**

- **Grouped vesicles on an erythematous base** → pathognomonic for herpes simplex (or zoster); vesicles are intraepidermal.
- **Preceding tingling/burning** → prodromal symptom due to viral reactivation and nerve irritation (ganglion involvement).
- **Recurrent episodes** → latent virus in sacral ganglia reactivates periodically.
- **Sexually active + new partner** → typical epidemiology for genital HSV-2 (though HSV-1 increasingly causes genital herpes).
- **Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy** → local lymphatic spread; supports diagnosis and helps differentiate from painless chancres.
- **Shallow ulcers after vesicle rupture** → typical natural history; ulcers heal without scarring in immunocompetent patients.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Neonatal herpes** – vesicular rash in a newborn, but may present with fever, irritability, or sepsis-like picture without skin lesions in visceral/CNS forms. Maternal history often absent.
- **Eczema herpeticum** – widespread vesicles on pre-existing eczematous skin in patients with atopic dermatitis. Can be mistaken for worsening eczema.
- **Herpetic whitlow** – isolated painful vesiculopustular lesion on a finger (often in healthcare workers). Misdiagnosed as paronychia or bacterial felon.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Herpes simplex is a double-stranded DNA virus (HSV-1 and HSV-2) that causes primary infection of mucosal or abraded skin, then establishes lifelong latency in sensory ganglia. Reactivation leads to recurrent lesions at or near the original site.

Why it matters:

- **Extremely common** – Estimated 67% of people under 50 have HSV-1 (mostly oral), 13% have HSV-2 (mostly genital). Many are asymptomatic but shed virus.
- **Neonatal herpes** – rare but devastating (mortality up to 50% without treatment); requires immediate IV acyclovir.
- **Immunocompromised patients** – extensive, atypical, or chronic ulcerations; risk of dissemination.
- **Misdiagnosis** – oral herpes can be mistaken for aphthous ulcers (no vesicles), genital herpes for syphilis or chancroid; incorrect treatment can delay healing and increase transmission.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Primary infection:** The virus enters through breaks in mucosa or skin. It replicates in epithelial cells, causing ballooning degeneration and lysis – this produces the characteristic **vesicle** (intraepidermal blister). The vesicle roof is thin and quickly breaks, leaving a **shallow ulcer**.

**Spread to nervous system:** From the epithelium, virus enters sensory nerve endings and travels retrograde to the dorsal root ganglion (trigeminal for oral, sacral for genital). Here it becomes **latent** – no virus replication, but viral DNA persists indefinitely.

**Reactivation triggers:** Fever, UV light, menstruation, stress, trauma, or immunosuppression reactivate the virus. It travels anterograde back to the skin, causing identical lesions at the same site.

**Why the prodrome?** Before lesions appear, viral reactivation in the ganglion triggers neuropathic pain/tingling. This is a key early clue.

**Why recurrence?** Latency means the virus cannot be eliminated. Immune control (especially T-cell responses) keeps it suppressed, but periodic reactivation occurs even in healthy hosts.

**Why lymphadenopathy?** Viral replication in skin releases antigens that drain to regional lymph nodes, causing inflammation and enlargement.

**Neonatal transmission:** Typically during vaginal delivery through contact with infected genital secretions. Can lead to disseminated disease (liver, lung, brain) or isolated CNS disease.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Direct infection** | HSV-1 (typically oral), HSV-2 (typically genital) | Lesion site matches serotype pattern (though overlap increasing) |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Fever, sunlight, stress, menstruation, trauma | Patient reports “cold sore” after sunburn or exam stress |
| **Immunocompromised state** | HIV, chemotherapy, transplant, steroids | Chronic, extensive, or atypical ulcers; also high risk for acyclovir resistance |
| **Neonatal acquisition** | Maternal genital herpes at time of delivery | Newborn with vesicles, fever, or encephalitis within 2 weeks of life (type may be HSV-2) |
| **Iatrogenic / occupational** | Needlestick injury, surgery (e.g., trigeminal nerve procedures) | Healthcare worker with finger lesion – herpetic whitlow |

**Most common cause:** HSV-1 for oral herpes (90%+); HSV-2 for genital herpes (60–70% in developed countries, but HSV-1 genital rising to 40% in some populations due to oral-genital contact).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is often sufficient – grouped vesicles on erythematous base + recurrence. Confirmation is recommended in: first episode (to type), severe/atypical cases, pregnancy, immunocompromised, neonates.

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Viral culture** (swab from vesicle base) | Live virus in cell culture | Gold standard for typing; high specificity | Positive = active infection. Negative may be false if lesion is drying. |
| **PCR** (swab or CSF) | Viral DNA | Most sensitive; rapid; can type (HSV-1 vs 2) | Positive confirms. Useful for CSF in suspected encephalitis or neonatal disease. |
| **Tzanck smear** (scraping from vesicle base) | Multinucleated giant cells (syncytia) | Quick bedside test; cheap | Positive suggests herpes (any type) – cannot differentiate HSV from VZV. Low sensitivity. |
| **Serology** (IgG / IgM) | Antibodies to HSV | History of recurrent infection? Determine past exposure (type-specific) | IgM not useful for acute diagnosis. IgG positive indicates past infection. Rising IgG in paired sera can confirm primary. Useful in pregnancy if partner has herpes. |
| **CSF analysis** (if encephalitis suspected) | Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose; HSV PCR positive | Confirm HSV encephalitis | High viral load in CSF correlates with worse outcome. |

**Single finding that confirms:** Positive HSV PCR from a lesion or CSF. Clinical appearance (grouped vesicles) combined with positive PCR is essentially 100% specific.

**Single finding that rules out:** Negative HSV PCR from a well-sampled vesicle (especially if fresh) in an immunocompetent patient with typical lesions – consider alternative diagnoses (aphthous ulcer, zoster, etc.).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**

- **Assess severity:** Is patient immunocompromised? Pregnant? Neonate? Disseminated? Encephalitis?
- **If severe or complicated** – start intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours (neonates: 20 mg/kg every 8 hours) – covers HSV, reduces viral load, prevents progression.
- **If mild-moderate first episode** – oral antiviral (see below) reduces duration and severity.
- **Pain control:** simple analgesia (paracetamol, ibuprofen). Topical lidocaine 2% gel can be used for mucosal lesions.
- **Wound care:** Keep lesions clean and dry. Avoid tight clothing for genital lesions.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**

- **Antiviral therapy (oral) for first episode:**
  - Acyclovir 400 mg orally three times daily for 5–10 days OR
  - Valacyclovir 1 g orally twice daily for 5–10 days OR
  - Famciclovir 250 mg orally three times daily for 5–10 days.
- **Recurrent episodes (mild):** Can be treated with episodic therapy (short course) or suppressive therapy (if frequent).
  - Episodic: Valacyclovir 500 mg twice daily for 3 days; start at prodrome.
  - Suppressive (≥6 episodes/year): Valacyclovir 500 mg once daily (or 1 g if frequent genital HSV-2). Reduces recurrences by 70–80%.
- **Neonatal herpes:** IV acyclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days (depending on disease extent).
- **HSV encephalitis:** IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 14–21 days; monitor renal function.
- **Pregnancy:** At term, consider prophylaxis (acyclovir 400 mg three times daily from 36 weeks) to reduce recurrence. Cesarean section recommended if active lesions at delivery.
- **HIV co-infection:** May need higher doses or longer courses; consider resistance testing if not improving.

**Long-term:**

- **Lifelong latency** – no cure. Focus on education: recognize prodrome, use condoms (reduces but does not eliminate transmission), avoid sexual contact during lesions.
- **Suppressive therapy** for frequent or severe recurrences (e.g., valacyclovir 500–1000 mg daily) – safe for long-term use.
- **Psychological support** – stigma is common; reassure that most people have HSV.

**What NOT to do:**

- **Do NOT use topical acyclovir for genital herpes** – oral therapy is far more effective. Topical is only an adjunct for oral herpes (and even then, oral is better).
- **Do NOT use steroids topically on herpes lesions** – can worsen and spread infection.
- **Do NOT assume all recurrent mouth ulcers are herpes** – aphthous ulcers are not vesicular and usually not painful at rest.
- **Do NOT delay acyclovir in suspected neonatal herpes** while waiting for culture – start IV acyclovir immediately.
- **Do NOT prescribe acyclovir for asymptomatic viral shedding without lesions** – no evidence of benefit for transmission reduction (unless partner is pregnant or immunocompromised – then discuss counseling).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Herpes Simplex (Oral/Genital) | Aphthous Ulcer (Oral) | Herpes Zoster (Shingles) | Primary Syphilis (Chancre) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Sharp, burning, often preceded by tingling | Sharp, stinging; no prodrome | Burning, shooting, dermatomal pain before rash | Painless (may be mildly tender) |
| **Exam** | Grouped vesicles on erythematous base → shallow ulcers | Solitary or multiple round/oval ulcers with white/yellow base, red halo, no vesicles | Vesicles in dermatomal distribution (usually unilateral); crusts later | Single, painless, indurated ulcer with clean base; unilateral lymphadenopathy (rubbery) |
| **Labs** | HSV PCR positive; multinucleated giant cells on Tzanck | No specific test; biopsy if suspicious of Behçet’s | VZV PCR positive; Tzanck also shows multinucleated cells | Dark-field microscopy shows spirochetes; serology positive (VDRL/RPR/TPPA) |
| **Key differentiator** | Recurrence at same site; grouped vesicles | Recurrent but never vesicular; no systemic symptoms | Dermatomal distribution, unilateral, severe pain, often in older/immunocompromised | Painless ulcer + history of exposure; test for syphilis |
| **Immunocompromised** | Extensive, chronic ulcerations; acyclovir resistance possible | May be larger and more painful; no vesicles | Disseminated zoster; postherpetic neuralgia common | Atypical rashes, multiple chancres |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Neonatal herpes** (missed maternal lesions) | Newborn (day 3–14) with vesicles, lethargy, fever, or seizures | Vesicular rash; may have no rash (CNS) | Start IV acyclovir immediately; perform CSF PCR; consult pediatric ID |
| **Herpes encephalitis** | Fever, headache, confusion, focal neurological deficits (temporal lobe) | CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, RBCs may be present. MRI: temporal lobe T2 hyperintensity | IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h; EEG shows periodic lateralizing epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) |
| **Disseminated HSV** | Multi-organ failure, hepatitis, pneumonitis | Elevated transaminases, coagulopathy, ARDS; skin lesions may be absent | IV acyclovir; requires ICU; consider resistance testing |
| **Eczema herpeticum** | Rapid onset of painful vesicles over pre-existing eczema; fever, malaise | Widespread vesicles, may become pustular; risk of secondary bacterial infection | IV acyclovir; stop topical steroids; consider antibiotics if superinfected |
| **Herpetic whitlow** | Painful vesiculopustular lesion on finger, often in healthcare workers | Misdiagnosed as bacterial felon | Oral valacyclovir 1 g twice daily for 7–10 days; avoid incision and drainage (spreads virus) |
| **HSV keratitis** (ocular herpes) | Pain, photophobia, blurred vision; dendritic ulcer on slit lamp | Dendritic ulcer on cornea (pathognomonic) | Urgent ophthalmology referral; topical antivirals (e.g., acyclovir ointment); steroids contraindicated in epithelial disease |
| **Immunocompromised chronic ulcers** | Non-healing ulcers >1 month | Large, painful, raised borders; may be acyclovir-resistant | Consider IV foscarnet or cidofovir; ID consultation |
| **Transmission to partner** | Asymptomatic shedding accounts for most transmission | No lesions, but virus detectable by PCR | Suppressive therapy reduces shedding; counseling on condoms and disclosure |

**Late-presentation patterns (what happens if untreated):**  
- Primary genital herpes can last 2–4 weeks with viral shedding and severe pain → risk of secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis).  
- Herpes encephalitis untreated → rapid progression to coma, brain herniation, death within days.  
- Neonatal herpes untreated → mortality >80%; survivors often have severe neurological deficits.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **"SORES" for features of herpetic ulcer:**  
  S – Shallow ulcer  
  O – On red base  
  R – Recurrent  
  E – Epithelial (vesicle then ulcer)  
  S – Sheds virus (contagious)

- **"ACID" for antiviral choice in genital herpes (first episode, mild):**  
  A – Acyclovir 400 mg TDS  
  C – Cycles 5–10 days  
  I – Interferon (not used)  
  D – Don't forget valacyclovir/famciclovir as easier alternatives

- **"NEONATE" risk factors for neonatal herpes:**  
  N – No maternal history (often first episode)  
  E – Early delivery (before 38 weeks)  
  O – Obvious lesions at delivery  
  N – Not using acyclovir prophylaxis  
  A – Asymptomatic shedding  
  T – Type (HSV-2 more neurovirulent)  
  E – Emergency C-section

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1. **Ulcers in the mouth that start as blisters? Think herpes – aphthous ulcers never have a vesicular stage.**  
2. **Genital ulcer that is painful = likely herpes or chancroid; painless = syphilis until proven otherwise.**  
3. **Recurrent rash on same finger in a nurse/dentist = herpetic whitlow until proven otherwise.**  
4. **Encephalitis with temporal lobe involvement + CSF RBCs = HSV until proven otherwise.**  
5. **Immunocompromised patient with chronic genital ulcers not healing with acyclovir → check for acyclovir resistance (usually in HIV with low CD4).**  
6. **First episode genital herpes can present with urinary retention (sacral radiculopathy) – catheterize and start antivirals.**  
7. **Neonatal rash plus lethargy: consider HSV even if no maternal history; start acyclovir immediately – do not wait for cultures.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- Mistaking herpetic gingivostomatitis (primary oral HSV in children) for hand-foot-mouth disease (coxsackievirus) – HFMD has vesicles on palms/soles and no fever in prodrome.
- Prescribing topical steroids for presumed eczema when it's eczema herpeticum (Kaposi's varicelliform eruption).
- Ordering HSV serology for acute lesions – PCR is faster and more sensitive.
- Starting antibiotics for a herpes lesion – they do not treat viral infection and may cause superinfection.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior or involve other specialties:**

- **Suspected neonatal herpes** – immediate consult to pediatrician, pediatric ID, start IV acyclovir.
- **HSV encephalitis** – call neurology/ID; MRI brain, EEG, CSF PCR; ICU if GCS < 8 or airway compromise.
- **Disseminated HSV (hepatitis, pneumonitis, DIC)** – discuss with ICU and ID; monitor transaminases, INR, oxygen saturations.
- **Ocular herpes (dendritic ulcer)** – urgent ophthalmology review within hours.
- **Immunocompromised patient not responding to empiric acyclovir** – discuss ID for resistance testing (foscarnet therapy).
- **Pregnant woman at term with active genital lesions** – call obstetrics: plan C-section, start IV acyclovir, neonatology consult.

**Specific thresholds to trigger escalation:**

- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially if encephalopathic) – generate sepsis/neurological evaluation.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (can occur in disseminated HSV hepatitis).
- **GCS < 15 with fever and headache** – suspect encephalitis, call neurologist.
- **Altered mental status + seizures** – immediate neurology/ICU involvement.
- **Rising transaminases (ALT > 500) without clear cause** – consider HSV hepatitis in at-risk patients (immunocompromised, pregnant).
